1.title: The Song of the Bow
1.text: David's lament for Saul and Jonathan was called 'the Bow' and was taught to the children of Judah. From it comes the phrase 'How are the mighty fallen!'

1.question: Who brought David the news of Saul's death?
options: An Amalekite who had fled the battle

1.question: What did the Amalekite claim, hoping for David's favor?
options: That he himself had killed Saul
That he had beaten the Philistines

1.question: What did David do with the man who boasted of killing the Lord's anointed?
options: He had him put to death

1.question: What did David compose on learning of Saul and Jonathan's deaths?
What famous phrase marks David's lament?
For whom did David express deep friendship in the lament?

2.title: Hebron, the first capital
2.text: David reigned over Judah at Hebron for seven and a half years before becoming king over all Israel and moving the capital to Jerusalem.

2.question: Where was David anointed king over Judah?
Whom did Abner make king over Israel, David's rival?
What happened by the pool of Gibeon?
options: A battle between David's men and Ish-bosheth's

2.question: Whom did Abner kill in the pursuit, Joab's brother?
What did Abner ask Joab at the end of the day of fighting?
options: That the slaughter between brothers cease
That they fight to the end

2.question: Who suffered far more casualties in that battle?
How many of David's men died, besides Asahel?

3.title: A long war
3.text: The war between the house of David and the house of Saul lasted a long time; little by little David grew stronger and Saul's house weaker.

3.question: At the chapter's start, how stood the war between the two houses?
options: David grew stronger and Saul's house weaker

3.question: What led Abner to turn to David's side?
What condition did David require of Abner for a covenant?
options: That he bring Michal back to him

3.question: What did Abner go to do among the tribes of Israel?
options: Persuade them to accept David as king

3.question: What did Joab think of the peace Abner made with David?
options: He distrusted it and accused him of spying

3.question: How did Joab kill Abner?
options: Treacherously, at the gate of Hebron, avenging Asahel

3.question: How did David react to Abner's death?
options: He mourned and fasted, showing he had not ordered it

3.question: What did the people understand from David's mourning for Abner?
options: That the king was not guilty of the death
That the war would go on

4.title: David builds on no man's blood
4.text: For the third time David punishes those who kill to please him (the Amalekite, and now Ish-bosheth's murderers): his kingdom is not built on treachery.

4.question: How was Ish-bosheth after Abner's death?
options: He grew weak, and Israel was troubled

4.question: What did two captains do to Ish-bosheth?
options: They killed him in his sleep and brought his head to David

4.question: What did David do to Ish-bosheth's murderers?

5.title: The city of David
5.text: By capturing the stronghold of Zion from the Jebusites, David made Jerusalem his capital; it came to be called 'the city of David.'

5.question: What did all the tribes of Israel do with David at Hebron?
options: They anointed him king over all Israel

5.question: What city did David capture and make his capital?
Why did David grow greater and greater?
options: Because the Lord of hosts was with him

5.question: Who helped David build his palace?
What did David do before attacking the Philistines?
What sign did God give David to attack, in the tops of the mulberry trees?
options: A sound of marching in the treetops

6.title: The ark is no common object
6.text: The Law required that only the Levites carry the ark, with poles. Transporting it on a new cart, like the Philistines, cost Uzzah his life.

6.question: What did David want to bring to Jerusalem?
What happened to Uzzah when he put out his hand to the ark?
options: He was struck dead by God on the spot

6.question: Where did the ark stay three months, blessing the house?
What did David do as he brought the ark, full of joy?
options: He danced before the Lord with all his might

6.question: Why did Michal despise David?
options: For dancing humbly before the people

7.title: The Davidic covenant
7.text: The promise of a throne established forever points to Jesus Christ, the 'Son of David,' whose kingdom will have no end (see Luke 1).

7.question: What did David want to build for the Lord?
options: A temple (a house) for the ark

7.question: What did God answer David through the prophet Nathan?
options: That David would not build the house, but God would make David a 'house' (dynasty)

7.question: Who would build the temple, according to the promise?
What everlasting promise did God make to David's house?
options: That his throne would be established forever
That he would live a thousand years

7.question: What did David do on hearing God's promise?
options: He prayed in gratitude before the Lord

7.question: What did David acknowledge about Israel in his prayer?
options: That God had redeemed it from Egypt as his own people
That it was the greatest empire
That he did not need God

7.question: Concerning what did David ask that God's word be fulfilled?

8.title: The kingdom's peak
8.text: The chapter lists David's officials (Joab over the army, etc.) and shows the kingdom at its greatest extent, under 'judgment and justice.'

8.question: What does chapter 8 summarize about David?
What did David do with the silver and gold taken in war?
options: He dedicated it to the Lord

8.question: How did David reign over all Israel?
options: With judgment and justice for all the people

9.title: Loyalty that keeps the promise
9.text: David sought someone of Saul's house to bless — not out of interest, but to keep the covenant of friendship he had made with Jonathan.

9.question: For whose sake did David want to show kindness to Saul's house?
Who was Mephibosheth, whom David welcomed?
options: Jonathan's son, lame in his feet

9.question: What did David grant Mephibosheth?
options: Saul's lands and a place at his table, like a king's son

10.title: An insult that sparked war
10.text: Shaving off half the beard and cutting the messengers' garments was a severe humiliation; it was the spark of war between Israel and Ammon.

10.question: Why did David send messengers to the king of Ammon?
options: To comfort him over his father's death

10.question: How did Hanun treat David's messengers?
options: He humiliated them, shaving their beards and cutting their garments
He welcomed them with a feast

10.question: What did the Ammonites do, fearing David's reaction?
options: They hired the Syrians to help them

10.question: What was the result of the wars against Ammon and Syria?

11.title: The danger of idleness
11.text: 'At the time when kings go forth to battle,' David stayed in Jerusalem. Idleness and abuse of power led him to adultery and murder.

11.question: What did David do when he saw Bath-sheba, Uriah's wife?
options: He had her brought and committed adultery

11.question: Why did Uriah not go home to rest when David summoned him?
options: Out of loyalty — he would not rest while the ark and army were in the field

11.question: What did David write in the letter carried by Uriah himself to Joab?
options: To set Uriah at the front of the battle, so he would die

11.question: How did Uriah die?
options: In battle, abandoned at the front line

11.question: How does the chapter end, after David took Bath-sheba as wife?
options: 'The thing that David had done displeased the Lord'

12.title: David's repentance
12.text: When confronted, David confessed: 'I have sinned against the Lord.' Psalm 51 is traditionally his prayer of repentance; he was forgiven, but the consequences remained.

12.question: What parable did Nathan tell David?
options: The rich man who took the poor man's only lamb

12.question: What did Nathan say, applying the parable to David?
What consequence did Nathan announce for David's house?
options: That the sword would not depart from it
That he would lose the throne at once

12.question: What did David say when confronted by Nathan?
How did David react when the sick child died?
options: He arose, washed, and worshiped the Lord

12.question: What son of David and Bath-sheba was born afterward, loved by the Lord?
What city did Joab take, and David received the king's crown?

13.title: The sword in David's house
13.text: From here Nathan's words come true: violence and tragedy strike David's own family, beginning with Amnon and Tamar.

13.question: With whom did Amnon, David's son, become sickly infatuated?
How did Amnon lure Tamar close to him?
options: He feigned illness and asked her to serve him
He invited her to a feast

13.question: What did Amnon do to Tamar?
options: He drove her from the palace

13.question: How did Amnon treat Tamar afterward?
options: He came to hate her and sent her away
He gave her half the kingdom

13.question: How did Absalom react to what Amnon had done to his sister?
options: He kept silent, but came to hate Amnon

13.question: What did Absalom do two years later, at a sheepshearing feast?
options: He had his servants kill Amnon

13.question: What exaggerated report first reached David?
options: That all his sons had been killed

13.question: Where did Absalom flee after killing Amnon?

14.title: Absalom's hair
14.text: Absalom was famed for his beauty and his very heavy head of hair, which he cut once a year — a detail that would play a tragic part in his death.

14.question: Who devised a plan to bring Absalom back from exile?
Whom did Joab send to David with a parable, to move him?
What did the woman of Tekoah obtain from David?
What condition did David set in letting Absalom return to Jerusalem?
options: That he would not see the king's face

14.question: What was Absalom especially known for in Israel?
options: His beauty and his thick head of hair

14.question: What did Absalom do to force Joab to receive him?
options: He had Joab's field set on fire

15.title: The prayer against Ahithophel
15.text: Ahithophel, a counselor famed for his wisdom, joined Absalom. David prayed: 'O Lord, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness' — and sent Hushai to do it.

15.question: How did Absalom steal the hearts of the people of Israel?
options: By flattering them at the gate and promising them justice

15.question: Where did Absalom proclaim himself king, starting the conspiracy?
What did David do on learning of Absalom's revolt?
options: He fled Jerusalem with his followers
He gathered the army in the city

15.question: Who insisted on following David, though a foreigner?
What did David tell Zadok to do with the ark of God?
Why did David not take the ark with him?
options: He trusted God would bring him back if it pleased Him

15.question: Whom did David send back to Jerusalem to thwart Ahithophel?

16.title: Nathan's word fulfilled
16.text: Ahithophel's counsel for Absalom to take his father's concubines in open sight fulfilled what Nathan had prophesied about the consequences of David's sin.

16.question: What did Ziba bring David, accusing Mephibosheth?
options: Provisions, saying Mephibosheth hoped to regain the kingdom

16.question: Who cursed David and threw stones during the flight?
How did David react to Shimei, who cursed him?
options: He bore it, saying perhaps the Lord had allowed it

16.question: What counsel did Ahithophel give Absalom to defy David?
options: To take publicly the concubines David had left
To seek an alliance with Egypt

17.title: Providence over the counsel
17.text: The text says 'the Lord had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel' — Absalom preferred Hushai's counsel, and it led him to ruin.

17.question: What counsel did Ahithophel give Absalom about David?
options: To pursue him at once, that very night

17.question: What contrary counsel did Hushai give?
options: To wait and gather all Israel first

17.question: Which counsel did Absalom end up following, to his ruin?
How was David warned to flee beyond the Jordan?
options: By messengers sent by the priests

17.question: What did Ahithophel do when his counsel was rejected?
Who sustained David with provisions at Mahanaim?

18.title: 'O my son Absalom!'
18.text: Despite his son's betrayal, David wept bitterly: 'would God I had died for thee, O Absalom, my son!' — one of the Bible's most moving laments.

18.question: What order did David give the captains about Absalom before the battle?
options: To deal gently with the young man

18.question: Where was the decisive battle against Absalom fought?
How was Absalom caught, defenseless?
options: By his head (hair) in an oak

18.question: Who killed Absalom, against David's order?
Who ran to bring David news of the battle?
How did David react on learning of Absalom's death?
options: He wept bitterly for his son

19.title: Joab's bluntness
19.text: Joab, though he had disobeyed by killing Absalom, rebuked David sharply: the king's excessive mourning was shaming the army that had saved him.

19.question: What did the army feel because of David's grief?
options: They were ashamed of the victory, as if they had lost

19.question: What did Joab demand of David?
options: That he rise and speak to the people, or be abandoned

19.question: What were the tribes debating after Absalom's defeat?
What did David do with Shimei, who had cursed him?
options: He pardoned him and swore not to kill him

19.question: What did Mephibosheth claim about not following David in the flight?
options: That Ziba had deceived and slandered him

19.question: How did Mephibosheth react when David divided the lands between him and Ziba?
options: He said Ziba could take it all, glad the king returned in peace

19.question: Who was Barzillai, who had helped David at Mahanaim?
What did Barzillai answer to David's invitation to live at court?
options: He declined, being very old, and named another in his place
He asked for half the kingdom

19.question: What conflict arose on the king's return to Jerusalem?
options: A dispute between Judah and Israel over who would bring the king

20.title: The wise woman of Abel
20.text: For the second time in the book, a 'wise woman' prevents a massacre: she negotiated with Joab, and the city of Abel was spared by handing over the rebel.

20.question: Who led a new revolt, saying 'we have no part in David'?
Whom did Joab treacherously kill during the pursuit of Sheba?
Where did Sheba take refuge, being besieged?
How was the city of Abel spared?
options: A wise woman negotiated, and they handed over Sheba's head

20.question: What does the end of the chapter list?

21.title: Rizpah's devotion
21.text: Rizpah watched over the bodies of Saul's sons for months, keeping off birds and beasts; her devotion moved David to honorably bury the bones of Saul and Jonathan.

21.question: What was the cause of the three-year famine in David's time?
What did the Gibeonites ask as restitution?
options: The handing over of Saul's descendants

21.question: What did Rizpah do by the bodies of Saul's sons?
options: She watched over them, keeping off birds and beasts

21.question: What does the chapter recount about the wars with the Philistines?
options: The killing of giants, kinsmen of Goliath

22.title: The song that is Psalm 18
22.text: This song of David is virtually identical to Psalm 18 — a poetic summary of a life marked by danger and the Lord's deliverance.

22.question: What is 2 Samuel chapter 22?
options: A song of David praising the Lord for delivering him from enemies

22.question: What images does David use for God at the start of the song?
To whom does David credit his deliverance?
options: To the Lord, who heard his cry

22.question: How does the song describe the Lord coming to the rescue?
Why did the Lord deliver David, according to the song?
What principle does David declare about God's dealings?
options: That he rewards according to righteousness and integrity

22.question: How does God treat the humble and the proud, according to the song?
options: He saves the humble and brings down the haughty

22.question: Who enabled David for battle?
options: God, who girded him with strength

22.question: What does David say about his enemies in the song?
options: That he overcame them by the Lord's strength

22.question: What does David promise in response to deliverance?
options: To praise and sing to the Lord's name among the nations

22.question: To whom does the song credit mercy forever?
options: To the Lord, toward David and his descendants

23.title: David's mighty men
23.text: Among 'the thirty' mighty men listed is Uriah the Hittite — a silent reminder of David's great sin, even in a chapter that celebrates heroes.

23.question: What are the opening verses of chapter 23?
Who were 'the three' and 'the thirty' named in the chapter?
What did one of the mighty men do alone in a field of lentils?
options: He defended it, and the Lord gave a great victory

23.question: What did three mighty men do for David at Beth-lehem?
options: They broke through the Philistine camp to bring him water from the well

23.question: What did David do with the water three men brought at the risk of their lives?
options: He poured it out to the Lord, refusing to drink it
He gave it to the army

23.question: What deeds of Benaiah does the chapter highlight?
options: He killed a lion in a pit and overcame an Egyptian

23.question: What does the end of the chapter list?
options: The names of David's mighty men

23.question: How many mighty men does David's list total at the end?
Who appears in the roll of mighty men, tied to the story of Bath-sheba?

24.title: 'I will not offer that which costs me nothing'
24.text: David refused to receive Araunah's threshingfloor for free: he bought it for the altar. That place, in Jerusalem, would become the mount where Solomon built the temple.

24.question: What sin did David commit, against Joab's counsel?
options: He numbered the people out of pride

24.question: What did David feel after numbering the people?
options: His heart smote him and he acknowledged the sin

24.question: What choice did the prophet Gad offer David as judgment?
options: Between famine, flight from enemies, or plague

24.question: What did David choose, and what came upon Israel?
options: To fall into the Lord's hand; a plague came

24.question: What did David buy to build an altar, where the plague stopped?
options: The threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite
